inheritance-for-shared-behavior.label: Inheritance for Shared Behavior
inheritance-for-shared-behavior.summary: Reuse parent setup, call it with `super()`, and extend inherited behavior without copying the parent code.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Build the shared CatalogItem parent
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: In `models/catalog_item.py`, complete `CatalogItem` so `__init__` stores `title` on `self.title` and `label(self)` returns the title. In `main.py`, create `CatalogItem("Hidden Figures")` and print its label. This gives the child classes shared setup to reuse next.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Complete the model first, then finish the small main script. The starter is intentionally unfinished.

help.concept: A parent/base class keeps shared setup in one place. Here, CatalogItem is the parent class that future catalog types can inherit from.
help.hint_1: The constructor should save the incoming `title` on `self`.
help.hint_2: The `label()` method should return that stored title.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.starterCode:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

# TODO: create one CatalogItem for "Hidden Figures".
# TODO: print the item label.

main_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

# TODO: create one CatalogItem for "Hidden Figures".
# TODO: print the item label.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.solutionCode:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

item = CatalogItem("Hidden Figures")
print(item.label())

main_py.content:
from models.catalog_item import CatalogItem

item = CatalogItem("Hidden Figures")
print(item.label())

2.message: Store the title on each CatalogItem object.

3.message: label() should return the stored title.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Call the parent constructor with super()
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: The `CatalogItem` parent already stores `title`. In `models/book.py`, make `Book` inherit from `CatalogItem`. Add `__init__(self, title, author)`, call `super().__init__(title)`, then store `author` on `self.author`. In `main.py`, create `Book("The Martian", "Andy Weir")` and print the title and author on separate lines.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Use `super().__init__(title)` before storing the Book-specific attribute.

help.concept: `super()` gives the child access to the next implementation in Python's inheritance chain. Calling the parent constructor avoids repeating shared setup.
help.hint_2: Inside Book.__init__, call `super().__init__(title)` and then assign `self.author = author`.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.starterCode:
from models.book import Book

# Create The Martian by Andy Weir, then print its title and author.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

# Create Book("The Martian", "Andy Weir").
# Print the inherited title and the Book-specific author.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Martian", "Andy Weir")
print(book.title)
print(book.author)

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Martian", "Andy Weir")
print(book.title)
print(book.author)

0.message: Define Book as a child of CatalogItem.

1.message: Book should accept title and author.

2.message: The parent constructor should provide title, and Book should store author.

3.message: Print the title and author on separate lines.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.title: Extend a parent method with super()
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.prompt: Add `label(self)` to `Book`. Start with `base_label = super().label()`, then return `f"{base_label} by {self.author}"`. In `main.py`, create `Book("The Hobbit", "J. R. R. Tolkien")` and print its label.
try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.hint: Call `super().label()` to reuse the parent result before adding the author.

help.concept: A child can override a method and still reuse the parent implementation. `super().label()` gets the parent result; the child adds its specialized detail.
help.hint_1: Keep the Book constructor that calls `super().__init__(title)`.
help.hint_2: Return the parent label followed by ` by ` and the author.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.starterCode:
from models.book import Book

# Create The Hobbit and print the extended label.

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

# Create Book("The Hobbit", "J. R. R. Tolkien").
# Print book.label().

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.solutionCode:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Hobbit", "J. R. R. Tolkien")
print(book.label())

main_py.content:
from models.book import Book

book = Book("The Hobbit", "J. R. R. Tolkien")
print(book.label())

0.message: Book should accept title and author.

1.message: Book.label() should extend the parent label with the author.

2.message: Create the Book object in main.py.

try_inheritance_for_shared_behavior_sket.expectedOutput: The Hobbit by J. R. R. Tolkien

sc-inheritance-shared-work.title: Choose why a base class helps here
sc-inheritance-shared-work.prompt: Book and Movie both need the same `title` setup. Why is a base class helpful?
sc-inheritance-shared-work.hint: Pick the answer about reusing shared code.

help.concept: Inheritance is helpful when related classes share the same core behavior.
help.hint_1: Look for the choice about storing shared setup once.
help.hint_2: Avoid answers about unrelated classes or identical output forever.

options.a: It prevents any subclass from having its own behavior.
options.b: It stores shared setup once so related subclasses can reuse it.
options.c: It turns every object into the same subclass.
options.d: It removes the need to create objects.

fb-inheritance-subclass-name.title: Call the parent constructor
fb-inheritance-subclass-name.prompt: Fill in the function used to reach the parent constructor.
fb-inheritance-subclass-name.hint: It returns access to the next class in the inheritance chain.

help.concept: `super()` lets a child call a parent implementation without naming the parent class directly.

dr-inheritance-build-order.title: Order a child constructor
dr-inheritance-build-order.prompt: Put these steps in a clear order.
dr-inheritance-build-order.hint: Connect the child to the parent before adding child-only state.

help.concept: The child class inherits first, then its constructor calls the parent setup, then it stores specialized state.
help.hint_2: Call super before using the completed object.

tokens.t1: Define the child class with the parent in parentheses

sc-inheritance-method-reuse.title: What does super() do here?
sc-inheritance-method-reuse.hint: The child wants to reuse the parent result and add more detail.

help.concept: `super()` can call the parent method as part of an override.
help.hint_1: The child does not need to copy the parent logic.
help.hint_2: It can extend the returned text.

options.a: It gets the parent label so Book can extend it.
options.d: It turns label into a static method.

sketch-0.title: Put shared setup in the parent
sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
Inheritance is useful when related classes share state or behavior.

```python
class Vehicle:
    def __init__(self, make):
        self.make = make
```

The parent owns the setup every child needs. Child classes can add only what makes them different.

Before introducing a parent class, identify behavior that is truly identical across the child types. Inheritance should remove meaningful duplication while leaving each subclass’s special state and output visible.

sketch-1.title: Use super() to run the parent constructor
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
When a child defines its own constructor, call the parent constructor for the shared part.

```python
class ElectricCar(Vehicle):
    def __init__(self, make, battery_level):
        super().__init__(make)
        self.battery_level = battery_level
```

`super().__init__(make)` runs the next constructor in Python's inheritance chain. The child avoids copying `self.make = make`.

Before introducing a parent class, identify behavior that is truly identical across the child types. Inheritance should remove meaningful duplication while leaving each subclass’s special state and output visible.

sketch-2.title: A child can extend a parent method
